meta.name: DJGLS De Jong Gierveld Loneliness Scale
meta.description: The DJGLS is a 6-item brief scale measuring emotional and social loneliness, widely used internationally.
meta.author: De Jong Gierveld & Van Tilburg (2006)
meta.reference: De Jong Gierveld J, Van Tilburg T. A 6-item scale for overall, emotional, and social loneliness. Research on Aging. 2006;28(5):582-598.
meta.instruction: Please rate how much you agree with each statement.

questions.text: I experience a general sense of emptiness
I miss having people around me
There are plenty of people I can rely on when I have problems
There are many people I can trust completely
There are enough people I feel close to

ranges.label: Low Loneliness
ranges.description: Your loneliness level is low, indicating healthy emotional bonds and adequate social connections. You feel supported by those around you and experience a sense of belonging in your relationships.
suggestions: Continue nurturing your existing friendships and family connections through regular meaningful interactions
Maintain your current social engagement habits that contribute to your emotional well-being
Consider offering support to others who may be experiencing loneliness, as helping strengthens bonds

ranges.label: Moderate Loneliness
ranges.description: You are experiencing moderate loneliness, suggesting some gaps in either emotional intimacy or social contact. While you may have some connections, certain relational needs may not be fully met at this time.
suggestions: Reach out proactively to friends or family members you have not spoken with recently to rebuild connections
Consider joining community groups, clubs, or classes aligned with your interests to expand your social network
Practice openly expressing your emotional needs to people you trust rather than keeping feelings to yourself
If loneliness persists despite your efforts, consider talking to a counselor about strategies for deeper connection

ranges.label: High Loneliness
ranges.description: Your loneliness level is high, indicating significant deficits in both emotional closeness and social integration. This degree of loneliness may be negatively affecting your mood, sleep, and overall quality of life.
suggestions: Actively expand your social network by participating in community events, volunteer work, or group activities
Seek opportunities for meaningful one-on-one conversations rather than relying solely on surface-level interactions
Consider joining a peer support group where you can share experiences and build connections with others
Consult a mental health professional to explore underlying patterns that may contribute to feelings of isolation
Monitor whether your loneliness is accompanied by symptoms of depression or anxiety that may need attention

ranges.label: Severe Loneliness
ranges.description: Your loneliness is severe, suggesting profound emotional and social isolation that may be significantly impairing your mental health and daily functioning. Professional support is strongly recommended.
suggestions: Seek professional support from a therapist or counselor experienced in addressing social isolation and loneliness
Make a deliberate effort to interact with at least one person each day, even through brief conversations
Consider psychotherapy to identify and modify relational patterns that may be maintaining your isolation
Monitor closely for co-occurring depression or anxiety symptoms and report them to a healthcare provider
